title: The neuroscience behind why journaling actually helps
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your hand is moving across the page. That's not nothing.

I've been journaling on and off for about fifteen years. And for most of that time, I had no idea why it sometimes helped and sometimes made things worse. I'd sit down with my notebook, full of good intentions, and end up either writing a grocery list or spiraling into a three-page rant about something my mother said in 1998. Neither felt particularly healing.

But here's the thing. Over the last few years, as I've studied trauma and nervous system science, I've started to understand what's actually happening in the brain when we write. And it's not what the wellness influencers tell you. It's not about "ing" or "positive affirmations" or any of that shiny nonsense. It's about wiring. It's about the actual physical architecture of your brain and how it processes experience.

Let me be clear about something right now. I'm not going to tell you that journaling will fix your trauma. It won't. Trauma is stored in the body, in the nervous system, in places that words can't always reach. But journaling can do something specific and measurable. It can help your brain make sense of experience. And for people living with complex trauma, that's not a small thing.

Stay with me here. I want to walk you through what the neuroscience actually says.

What happens in your brain when you write

You've probably heard of the amygdala. It's that small almond-shaped structure deep in your brain that acts like a smoke detector. When it senses threat, it sets off alarms throughout your nervous system. Cortisol floods your system. Your heart rate increases. Your thinking brain, the prefrontal cortex, starts to shut down. This is the fight or flight response, and for trauma survivors, it can be triggered constantly.

Now here's where it gets interesting. When you write about an experience, especially a difficult one, you're engaging the left prefrontal cortex. This is the part of your brain responsible for language, logic, and narrative. And when that part of the brain is active, it can actually calm the amygdala down. Not completely. Not always. But often enough that researchers have measured the effect.

Matthew Lieberman, a neuroscientist at UCLA, has done some of the most important work on this. He found that putting feelings into words reduces activity in the amygdala. He calls this "affect labeling." Just naming an emotion changes how your brain processes it. Think about that. You don't have to solve anything. You don't have to fix anything. You just have to name it. And your brain starts to shift.

Think about it this way. Your brain developed in an environment where certain things were true. Maybe love came with conditions. Maybe safety was unpredictable. Maybe the people who were supposed to protect you were the same people your nervous system needed protection from. That creates a particular kind of wiring.

Sebern Fisher's work on neurofeedback shows how these patterns literally show up in brainwave activity. Hypervigilant brains produce excess high-beta waves. Dissociative brains show too much slow-wave activity in the wrong places. This isn't metaphor. This is measurable, observable, and - critically - changeable.

I remember the first time I saw my own brain map. Seeing the dysregulation in color-coded images on a screen. Something shifted in that moment. It wasn't shame. It was relief. Because if it's in the wiring, then it's not a character flaw. It's not weakness. It's not something I chose. And if it got wired this way through experience, then experience can rewire it. Slowly. Imperfectly. But really.

I remember the first time I tried this intentionally. I was sitting in my car after a therapy session, completely flooded. My hands were shaking. My chest felt tight. I pulled out my phone and opened a notes app. And I wrote: "I am terrified right now. My body feels like it's in danger. I don't know why." That was it. Three sentences. And within about sixty seconds, my hands stopped shaking. Not because I had figured anything out. But because my brain had a label for what was happening.

Wild, right?

The problem with most journaling advice

Here's where I have to get a little honest about the wellness industry. Most of the journaling advice out there is designed for people with regulated nervous systems. People who can sit down and write about their feelings without getting hijacked by their trauma. That's not most of us.

I've tried the gratitude journal thing. I've tried the "write your future self a letter" thing. I've tried the "morning pages" thing where you're supposed to write three pages of stream of consciousness every day. And you know what happened? I either felt worse because I couldn't do it consistently, or I felt worse because the act of writing stirred up stuff I wasn't ready to touch.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing involves moving between activation and settling, between the difficult material and a place of safety. Journaling can be part of that, but only if you know how to pendulate. Only if you know when to stop.

I want you to hear this. If journaling makes you feel worse, you're not doing it wrong. Your nervous system is telling you something important. Maybe you need a different approach. Maybe you need to write less. Maybe you need to write differently. Maybe you need to put the notebook down and go for a walk instead.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine's work on how trauma gets trapped in the body has been genuinely useful for me, and it might help you understand why talking or writing about trauma isn't always enough.

The difference between processing and retraumatizing

This is the part that most people don't talk about. There's a difference between processing an experience and retraumatizing yourself. And journaling can easily become the latter if you're not careful.

Here's what retraumatizing looks like in practice. You sit down to write about a traumatic memory. You describe it in detail. You feel the feelings again. Your heart races. Your breathing changes. Your body tenses. And then you close the notebook and go about your day, but your nervous system is still activated. You've replayed the trauma without any resolution. That's not healing. That's rehearsal.

Processing, on the other hand, involves some distance. Some perspective. Some ability to observe the experience rather than being consumed by it. This is where the brain's narrative capacity comes in. When you write about an experience from a slightly removed perspective, you're engaging what Dan Siegel calls the "observing self." You're watching the story unfold rather than being inside it.

One technique that helps me is to write in third person. Instead of "I was scared when he said that," I write "She was scared when he said that." It sounds strange, I know. But it creates enough distance that my nervous system doesn't go into full alarm mode. I can still access the emotion, but I'm not drowning in it.

Another technique is to time your writing. I set a timer for five minutes. That's it. When the timer goes off, I close the notebook. No matter where I am in the sentence. This creates a container. Your brain learns that the writing session has a beginning and an end. It's not an endless spiral.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk's research on how trauma affects the brain and body has shaped so much of how I understand my own experience. He talks about the importance of finding ways to process trauma that don't retraumatize you. Journaling can be one of those ways, but it has to be done thoughtfully.

What the research actually shows

There's a body of research on expressive writing that goes back to the 1980s. James Pennebaker, a psychologist at the University of Texas, pioneered much of this work. His studies showed that people who wrote about traumatic experiences for fifteen to twenty minutes a day for three to four days showed improvements in immune function, fewer doctor visits, and better psychological well-being.

But here's what gets left out of the Instagram posts. Pennebaker's studies were done with college students, not with people who have complex trauma. The writing was structured. It had specific instructions. And the effects were modest. Not . Not miraculous. Just measurable improvements.

More recent research has shown that expressive writing can help with things like working memory and emotional regulation. But it's not a substitute for therapy. It's not a substitute for somatic work. It's a tool. One tool among many.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry's book offers a perspective that has helped me be kinder to myself about my own history. Sometimes the most important thing journaling can do is help you reframe your story.

How to journal when your nervous system is dysregulated

I want to give you something practical. Something you can actually use. Because I know what it's like to want to journal and not be able to. To stare at a blank page and feel nothing. Or to stare at a blank page and feel too much.

Start small. I mean really small. One sentence. That's enough. "I woke up today." "I am tired." "I don't know what to write." That's a complete journal entry. Your brain doesn't need a novel. It needs a signal. It needs you to show up and acknowledge what's happening.

Use prompts that focus on the present moment. What do you notice in your body right now? What temperature is the room? What sounds do you hear? This kind of grounding writing engages the prefrontal cortex and helps regulate the nervous system. It's not about processing trauma. It's about building capacity.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). I take it sometimes before I write, especially if I'm feeling anxious. It helps take the edge off so I can actually think clearly. Not a cure, but a support.

Write for yourself. Not for an audience. Not for future publication. Not even for your therapist. Write for the part of you that needs to be heard. The part that doesn't get a voice in your daily life. The part that's been silenced by trauma, by shame, by the constant pressure to be okay.

And when you're done, close the notebook. Put it away. Do something that soothes your nervous system. Stretch. Breathe. Drink water. Pet your dog. The writing is only half of the process. The settling is the other half. Don't skip it.

I've been writing this article for about two hours now. My shoulders are tight. My eyes are tired. I need to take my own advice. So I'm going to close my laptop and go for a walk. The words will still be here when I get back. And so will you.
